repos.description: An R package providing a modular, extendable system for checking research outputs for best practices.
repos.summary: Recent development activity for the 'scienceverse/metacheck' repository has focused on refining the package's functionality, particularly with regards to import updates, testing, and documentation. Specifically, updates have been made to the grobid server, test coverage, and pkgdown releases, with multiple merge requests and fixes for various issues, such as failing tests and encoding problems. The overall direction of development suggests a push towards stabilizing and refining the package, with changes like updating from alpha to beta version and stopping auto tests, indicating a move towards a more mature release.

r_meta.description_text: A modular, extendable system for checking research outputs for best practices using text search, R code, and/or (optional) LLM queries.

repos.readme_excerpt:
![Made in Europe](https://img.shields.io/badge/Made_in_Europe-003399?logo=european-union&logoColor=FFCC00)






The goal of metacheck is to automatically check research outputs for best practices. You can find out more at <https://scienceverse.github.io/metacheck/>.


You can install the development version of metacheck from [GitHub](https://github.com/) with:

``` r
remotes::install_github("scienceverse/metacheck")
```



## Notes for Developers

You may not contribute any code unless you also contribute a test of this code.

Check tests/testthat/helper.R for custom test skip functions. All

r_meta.description_text: A "spellchecker" for statistics. It checks whether your p-values match their accompanying test statistic and degrees of freedom. statcheck searches for null-hypothesis significance test (NHST) in APA style (e.g., t(28) = 2.2, p < .05). It recalculates the p-value using the reported test statistic and degrees of freedom. If the reported and computed p-values don't match, statcheck will flag the res

repos.readme_excerpt:
`statcheck` is a “spellchecker” for statistics. It checks whether your
*p*-values match their accompanying test statistic and degrees of
freedom.

`statcheck` searches for null-hypothesis significance test (NHST) in APA
style (e.g., *t*(28) = 2.2, *p* \< .05). It recalculates the p-value
using the reported test statistic and degrees of freedom. If the
reported and computed p-values don’t match, `statcheck` will flag the
result as an error.

![](man/figures/infograph.png)


`statcheck` is mainly useful for:

1.  **Self-checks**: you can use `statcheck` to make sure your
    manuscript doesn’t c

recent_commits.diff_summary: This commit updates the 'statcheck' function in the 'MicheleNuijten/statcheck' repository to use the `message` function instead of `cat` when no results are found, and also updates several test cases to reflect this change, ensuring that the function's behavior is correctly tested when the 'respect.messages' option is set to FALSE.

releases.name: Maintenance release that provides minor fixes following the v0.4.0 feature update.
releases.body:
### Summary of v0.4.0 (previous major release)
- Added support for acknowledged contributors and unnamed contributors.
- Introduced separate contributor statements for authors and acknowledgees.
- Enhanced JATS 1.3 XML output with acknowledgees, funding, and conflict of interest statements.
- Ad

releases.name: Support for Acknowledged Contributors and ORCID
releases.body:
- Support for acknowledged contributors and those who do not want to be named
- Separate contributor statements for acknowledgees and authors
- Enhanced JATS 1.3 XML output with acknowledgees, funding, and conflict of interest statements
- ORCID badge integration with hyperlink or plain text opti

releases.name: 🚀 Custom Validation & New Outputs
releases.body:
Compared to the stable v0.1.0 release, this version introduces the following changes:

    ✅ New Output Options: Generate outputs for funding information and conflict of interest statements.
    🔍 Custom Validation: Enhanced input validation at the output level for more reliable data handling.

releases.name: Last version before deluxe column enhancement
releases.body: We are about to add more affiliation columns and a new validation system, so we are making this release in case anyone relies on the existing version.

releases.name: tenzing: documening contributorship with CRediT
releases.body: Tenzing is an easy-to-use web-based app and R package, that allows researchers to generate reports about the contribution of each team member on a project using CRediT, for insertion into their manuscripts and for publishers to potentially incorporate into article metadata.

r_meta.description_text: A modularized shiny app that reads contributorship information from a local source and produces human and machine-readable summaries.

repos.readme_excerpt:
[![Lifecycle:
experimental](https://img.shields.io/badge/lifecycle-experimental-orange.svg)](https://www.tidyverse.org/lifecycle/

Tenzing, an easy-to-use web-based app, allows researchers to generate
reports about the contribution of each team member on a project using
CRediT, for insertion into their manuscripts and for publishers to
potentially incorporate into article metadata.

[CRediT](http://credit.niso.org/) (Contributor Roles Taxonomy) is a
taxonomy of 14 categories of contributions to scientific scholarly
output. Each researcher can indicate which category they contributed to
in a sc

+    dplyr::mutate(
+      affiliation_no = dplyr::if_else(
+        is.na(affiliation),
+        NA_integ

@@ -73,10 +73,11 @@ print_yaml <- function(contributors_table) {
     dplyr::mutate(name = factor(name, levels = name))
   
   # Generate role assignments
+  credit_roles <- dplyr::pull(credit_taxonomy, `CRediT Taxonomy`)
   contrib_data$role <- I(
     purrr::map(
       split(contrib_data, contrib_data$name),
-      ~ names(dplyr::select(., dplyr::pull(credit_taxonomy, `CRediT Taxonomy`)))[.x[1, -c(1:4)] == TRUE]
+      ~ names(dplyr::select(., dplyr::all_of(credit_roles)))[.x[1, credit_roles]

@@ -10,7 +10,7 @@ mod_support_popup_server(
   enable = TRUE,
   show_prob = 0.33,
   delay_ms = 1500,
-  dismiss_ms = 15000,

   donation_url = "https://opencollective.com/tenzing"
 )
 }

releases.name: scrutiny 0.5.0
releases.body:
The package is now released under the MIT license.

## Breaking changes

-   The `ratio` column in the output of `grim_map()` and `grim_map_seq()` was replaced by a `probability` column. This means:

    -   Numerically, the only difference is that `probability` is zero whenever `ratio` was negative

releases.name: scrutiny 0.4.0
releases.body:
This version brings major performance improvements. Furthermore:

## Bugfixes

-   Fixed a bug in `audit_seq()`: If the `dispersion` argument in the preceding call to a function like `grim_map_seq()` was specified as something other than a linearly increasing sequence, the `"diff_*"` columns in the

releases.name: scrutiny 0.3.0
releases.body:
## Duplicate analysis overhaul

The `duplicate_*()` functions now present their output better and have overall been streamlined. Read more at `vignette("duplicates")`.

-   A new function, `duplicate_tally()`, marks each observation with its overall frequency. It is similar to `duplicate_detect()` b

# scrutiny 0.2.3

Some new features and bugfixes:

-   New `audit()` methods for the output of `audit_seq()` and `audit_total_n()`.

-   New `duplicate_count_colpair()` function that checks each combination of columns in a data frame for duplicates.

-   New `restore_zeros_df()` function to

releases.name: Release of scrutiny 0.2.2

"Releasing" the development version right now in order for the website (https://lhdjung.github.io/scrutiny/) to flag a bug in the GRIMMER implementation, concerning the `items` argument used for composed scales.

This is a patch fo

r_meta.title: Error Detection in Science
r_meta.authors: c( person(given = "Lukas", family = "Jung", role = c("aut", "cre"), email = "jung-lukas@gmx.net"), person(given = "Aurélien", family = "Allard", role = c("ctb")), person(given = "Nicolas Roman", family = "Posner", role = c("ctb")) )
r_meta.description_text: Test published summary statistics for consistency (Brown and Heathers, 2017, <doi:10.1177/1948550616673876>; Allard, 2018, <https://aurelienallard.netlify.app/post/anaytic-grimmer-possibility-standard-deviations/>; Heathers and Brown, 2019, <https://osf.io/5vb3u/>). The package also provides infrastructure for implementing new error detection techniques.

repos.readme_excerpt:
[![Codecov test
coverage](https://codecov.io/gh/lhdjung/scrutiny/branch/main/graph/badge.svg)](https://app.codecov.io/gh/lhdjung/scrutiny?branch=main)




The goal of scrutiny is to test published summary statistics for
consistency using techniques like GRIM and to check their plausibility.
The package makes these methods easy to use in a tidyverse-friendly way.
It hopes to help the new field of error detection go mainstream.

You can use the most important parts in the [Error
detection](https://errors.shinyapps.io/scrutiny) Shiny app instead.

Besides ready-made tests, scrutiny features a com

It is no longer sufficient for a citation or accesion number, or link to be in the same sentence as "was_available".
PDF to text conversion was improved to remove left margin text and deal

r_meta.description_text: Detection of Open Data & Open Code statements in biomedical publications. Uses publication PDFs as input and returns both Open Data/Code: 'TRUE/FALSE', the detected statement, as well as additional metadata.

repos.readme_excerpt:
ODDPub is a text mining algorithm that parses a set of publications and detects
which publications disseminated Open Data or Open Code together with the
publication. It is tailored towards biomedical literature.


Nico Riedel, Vladislav Nachev (vladislav.nachev@charite.de), Miriam Kip, Evgeny Bobrov (evgeny.bobrov@bih-charite.de) - QUEST Center for Transforming Biomedical Research, Berlin Institute of Health


More information on the development and validation of the algorithm can be found in the publication https://doi.org/10.5334/dsj-2020-042. The related training and validation datasets can

r_meta.description_text: Create reproducible and transparent research projects in 'R'. This package is based on the Workflow for Open Reproducible Code in Science (WORCS), a step-by-step procedure based on best practices for Open Science. It includes an 'RStudio' project template, several convenience functions, and all dependencies required to make your project reproducible and transparent. WORCS is explained in the tutor

recent_commits.diff_summary: This commit updates the 'worcs' package to version 0.1.21.1, adds a new vignette called 'create_project.Rmd' to guide users in creating a new project, and documents these changes in the package's news file.

repos.description: Extract and analyse statistical information from Word documents.
repos.summary: Recent development activity for the GitHub repository 'giladfeldman/docpluck' has focused on fixing various issues and implementing new features. Specifically, the developer has addressed problems with reference splitting, page breaks, and running header stripping, while also introducing new features such as first-class footnote texts API and extract_pdf_layout export. Additionally, the developer has worked on improving the documentation and testing of the project, including the implementation of a token-capture script for pre-test comparisons and the resolution of hallucinated heading guards. Overall, the development direction appears to be aimed at enhancing the accuracy and reliability of the document processing capabilities.

repos.readme_excerpt:
Detection and extraction of Author Contribution statements and Acknowledgements from scientific papers


RRID:SCR_027089

ContriBOT is a text mining algorithm that parses a set of publications and detects
which publications included Authorship Statements, Acknowledgements and ORCIDs.



Vladislav Nachev (vladislav.nachev@charite.de), Fyeqa Akram - BIH QUEST Center for Responsible Research


The latest version of the algorithm is structured as an R package and can easily be installed with the following command:

``` r

devtools

r_meta.title: Identifies indicators of transparency
r_meta.authors: c(person("Stylianos", "Serghiou", role = c("aut", "cre"), email = "stelios.serghiou@gmail.com"), person("Nachev", "Vladislav", role = c("aut"), email = "vladislav.nachev@charite.de") )
r_meta.description_text: Use this package to identify indicators of transparency within the published literature. It can identify and extract text related to indicators of transparency from specifically formatted TXT files and from PMC XML files (i.e. XML files downloaed from the PubMed Central).

repos.readme_excerpt:
<div align="justify">
  

`rtransparent` is an R package that automatically identifies and extracts indicators of transparency (data availability, code availability, statements of conflicts of interest, statements of funding, statements of protocol registration) from TXT and PMC XML versions of published research articles in biomedicine.



Stylianos (Stelios) Serghiou (sstelios@stanford.edu). This package also utilizes functions from the `oddpub` package of Nico Riedel (nico.riedel@bihealth.de).


## Publication

`rtransparent` was validated and subsequently used to extract indicators of tran

repos.summary: The GitHub repository 'Davidvandijcke/coarse' has seen recent development activity focused on bug fixes, new feature additions, and model updates. Specifically, the repository has been updated to include new models such as Claude Fable 5 and Opus 4.8, and has addressed various issues related to OpenRouter key handling, pipeline timeouts, and extraction errors. The overall direction of development appears to be aimed at improving the stability, functionality, and model offerings of the repository, with multiple releases (v1.5.0, v1.6.0, and v1.7.0) being pushed out in a short span of time.

releases.name: v1.1.3 — coarse-ink PyPI rename (fixes #17)
releases.body:
## What's new

First release under the new `coarse-ink` PyPI name. `uvx coarse-ink review paper.pdf ...` now works end-to-end.

### Fixed

- **PyPI distribution renamed to `coarse-ink`** — the bare `coarse` name on PyPI was already taken by an unrelated, abandoned package (`coarse==0.0.1`), so `uvx

releases.name: v1.1.0
releases.body:
## What's new

### Added
- **Multi-format support** — review .txt, .md, .tex, .docx, .html, and .epub files in addition to PDF. Docling (if installed) for high-quality conversion, with lightweight fallbacks. New optional dependency: `pip install coarse[formats]`.
- **No-retention provider routing**

repos.readme_excerpt:
Free, open-source AI academic paper reviewer that outperforms popular paid AI reviewers.

You provide your own API key and pay the LLM provider directly — typically **under $2 per review**.

Don't want to run it locally? Use the [web interface](https://coarse.vercel.app/) instead.


Get an API key from [OpenRouter](https://openrouter.ai/keys) (free to sign up), then:

```bash
uvx coarse-ink review paper.pdf --api-key sk-or-v1-YOUR_KEY
```

That's it. The review is written to `paper_review.md` in the current directory.


coarse requires Python 3.12+. If you don't have `uvx`, install [uv](https:

recent_commits.message: fix(web): stop demanding an OpenRouter key for non-PDF subscription handoffs (#186) (#217)
recent_commits.author: David Van Dijcke

files.patch:
@@ -2,6 +2,10 @@
 
 ## Unreleased
 
+### Fixed
+
+- **"Review with my subscription" no longer demands an OpenRouter key for non-PDF uploads (issue #186).** The handoff mechanics already ran `.tex`/`.md`/`.docx`/`.html`/`.epub`/`.txt` sources end-to-end with no OpenRouter key — only PDFs use Mistral OCR; everything else extracts locally, and the literature search quietly falls back to the free arXiv path — but every user-facing surface still demanded a key unconditionally. Worst, the agent prompt

@@ -61,12 +61,15 @@ Or run `coarse setup` to store keys in `~/.coarse/config.toml`.
 If you already have a `claude`, `codex`, or `gemini` CLI logged into its subscription,
 `coarse-review` routes every pipeline LLM call through the local CLI (`claude -p`,
 `codex exec`, `gemini -p`) so reviews bill your Claude Max / ChatGPT Pro / Google AI Pro
-subscription instead of a pay-per-token API. Only the ~$0.05–0.15 Mistral OCR step still
-uses `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` (skip it with `--pre-extracted paper.

@@ -14,7 +14,7 @@ description: >
 
 # coarse-review (Claude Code)
 
-Runs the **full coarse review pipeline** on a paper using the local `claude -p` CLI as the LLM backend. Every LLM call the pipeline makes — structure metadata, overview, per-section review, proof verification, editorial dedup — is served by a headless Claude Code subprocess using the user's Claude Max subscription. The only per-paper cost is the ~$0.05-0.15 Mistral OCR extraction, which uses the user's OpenRouter key locally.

recent_commits.diff_summary: This commit fixes an issue where the "Review with my subscription" feature was unnecessarily demanding an OpenRouter key for non-PDF subscription handoffs, and updates various documentation files to reflect this change.
